---
title: "Why Rust Is Hard (And Why It's Worth It)"
author: "Jon Gjengset"
author_url: "https://thesquareplanet.com"
date: 2026-05-10
type: video
category: "Programming Languages"
tags: [rust, memory-safety, systems-programming]
video_url: "https://www.youtube.com/embed/IrqklfvmAS0"
source_url: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IrqklfvmAS0"
estimated_read_time: 5
is_own_work: false
gists:
  quick: "Rust's difficulty comes from its ownership and borrowing system, which eliminates a whole class of memory bugs at compile time — bugs that account for ~70% of CVEs in systems software. The compiler rejects programs that would cause data races, use-after-free, or double-frees. Once you internalize the model, you gain not just safety but a clearer mental model of data lifetime and ownership that makes you a better programmer in any language."
  medium: "Rust is hard because it makes you reason explicitly about something most languages hide: who owns this memory, and for how long is it valid?\n\nIn C/C++, you have full control but the compiler won't stop you from using memory after you free it (use-after-free) or from letting two threads mutate the same data simultaneously (data race). These bugs are the source of ~70% of security vulnerabilities in large codebases (Microsoft, Google, NSA data all converge on this number).\n\nIn garbage-collected languages (Java, Python, Go), the runtime handles memory for you — but at the cost of unpredictable GC pauses, higher memory overhead, and no control over when objects are released.\n\nRust's ownership system is a third option: the compiler tracks ownership and lifetimes at compile time, guaranteeing memory safety without a runtime GC. The rules are: every value has exactly one owner, ownership can be transferred (moved) or borrowed (shared reference &T or exclusive reference &mut T), and references cannot outlive the data they point to (lifetimes).\n\nThe hardest concept is lifetimes — annotations that tell the compiler how long references are valid. Most of the time the compiler infers them, but when you write functions that return references, you must be explicit. This is where most learners get stuck.\n\nIn this video, Jon Gjengset (author of 'Rust for Rustaceans') walks through why these design choices were made and how to develop the mental model that makes Rust click."
  full: "Rust has a reputation as one of the hardest programming languages to learn, but also one of the most rewarding. This video by Jon Gjengset explains the why behind the difficulty — and why it's the right trade-off for systems programming.\n\n## The Problem Rust Solves\n\nMemory bugs — use-after-free, buffer overflow, double-free, data races — account for roughly 70% of security vulnerabilities in C and C++ codebases. This is not a matter of programmer skill; these bugs have been found in code written by the best engineers in the world, in operating systems, browsers, and cryptographic libraries. The C/C++ model gives you control but provides no guardrails.\n\nGarbage-collected languages (Java, Go, Python, C#) solve memory safety by delegating memory management to a runtime. This works well for most applications but introduces GC pauses, higher memory overhead, and gives up low-level control — making them unsuitable for OS kernels, device drivers, real-time systems, and high-performance computing.\n\n## Rust's Solution: Ownership\n\nRust introduces a compile-time ownership system with three rules:\n1. Every value has exactly one owner\n2. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped (memory freed)\n3. You can borrow a value via references, but references cannot outlive the data\n\nOwnership can be **moved** (the original variable becomes invalid) or **borrowed** via references — either shared (`&T`, multiple readers) or exclusive (`&mut T`, one writer, no readers).\n\nThis simple model eliminates:\n- **Use-after-free**: Can't use moved values; references can't outlive owners\n- **Double-free**: Only the owner frees; no way to free twice\n- **Data races**: Rust's type system enforces that `&mut T` is exclusive — impossible to have two threads with mutable access simultaneously\n- **Null pointer dereferences**: No null in Rust; use `Option<T>` instead\n\n## Lifetimes\n\nLifetimes are the hardest concept in Rust. A lifetime is an annotation that describes how long a reference is valid. Most of the time, the compiler infers them automatically (lifetime elision). But when a function returns a reference, you often need to explicitly annotate: 'this returned reference lives as long as this input reference.'\n\n```rust\n// The returned reference lives as long as the input\nfn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str {\n    if x.len() > y.len() { x } else { y }\n}\n```\n\nLifetimes are not about duration in wall-clock time — they're about the scope in which a reference is valid. Thinking of them as \"region annotations\" or \"validity scopes\" is more accurate.\n\n## Why It's Worth It\n\nOnce the ownership model clicks, you get:\n- **Zero-cost abstractions**: High-level code that compiles to the same machine code as hand-written C\n- **Fearless concurrency**: The type system prevents data races at compile time\n- **No GC**: Predictable performance, no pause-the-world collections\n- **Cross-compilation**: Build for embedded, WASM, and systems targets easily\n- **A better mental model**: Explicit ownership thinking makes you reason more carefully about data in any language\n\nRust has been the 'most loved language' in Stack Overflow's developer survey for nine consecutive years. Its difficulty is front-loaded — steep at the start, but pays off in programs that are faster, safer, and more maintainable.\n\n## Resources\n- *The Rust Programming Language* (the official book, free online)\n- *Rust for Rustaceans* by Jon Gjengset (intermediate to advanced)\n- Jon's YouTube channel (Crust of Rust series — excellent deep dives)"
---

This is one of Jon Gjengset's most accessible videos explaining the ownership system and lifetimes that make Rust unique — and why the compile-time guarantees are worth the learning curve.
